section.title: Zion's New Name
section.sectionRationale: This chapter answers the shame of a forsaken city with new names, Hephzibah, My Delight Is in Her, and Beulah, Married, and pictures God rejoicing over Zion the way a bridegroom rejoices over a bride. Watchmen are posted who will not rest until Jerusalem's praise fills the earth, and the chapter closes with a herald announcing her Savior comes.
section.thesis: God renames a city once called Forsaken and Desolate with names of delight and marriage, promising to rejoice over her as a bridegroom rejoices over his bride, and commissions restless watchmen and a highway-building people to prepare the way until He is seen to come.
section.frameNote: Three movements: a new name that the LORD's own mouth will bestow; no longer Forsaken but Hephzibah and Beulah; and prepare the way, for your salvation comes.

units.label: A new name from the LORD's own mouth
units.note: for Zion's sake I will not keep silent

verses.text: For Zion’s sake I will not keep silent, and for Jerusalem’s sake I will not keep still, until her righteousness shines like a bright light, her salvation like a blazing torch.
Nations will see your righteousness, and all kings your glory. You will be called by a new name that the mouth of the LORD will bestow.
You will be a crown of glory in the hand of the LORD, a royal diadem in the palm of your God.

units.label: Hephzibah and Beulah
units.note: as a bridegroom rejoices over his bride

verses.text: No longer will you be called Forsaken, nor your land named Desolate; but you will be called {{hephzibah|Hephzibah}}, and your land {{beulah|Beulah}}; for the LORD will take delight in you, and your land will be His bride.
For as a young man marries a young woman, so your sons will marry you; and as a bridegroom rejoices over his bride, so your God will rejoice over you.
On your walls, O Jerusalem, I have posted watchmen; they will never be silent day or night. You who call on the LORD shall take no rest for yourselves,
nor give Him any rest until He establishes Jerusalem and makes her the praise of the earth.
The LORD has sworn by His right hand and by His mighty arm: “Never again will I give your grain to your enemies for food, nor will foreigners drink the new wine for which you have toiled.
For those who harvest grain will eat it and praise the LORD, and those who gather grapes will drink the wine in My holy courts.”

units.label: Prepare the way, your salvation comes
units.note: Sought Out, A City Not Forsaken

verses.text: Go out, go out through the gates; prepare the way for the people! Build it up, build up the highway; clear away the stones; raise a banner for the nations!
Behold, the LORD has proclaimed to the ends of the earth, “Say to Daughter Zion: See, your Savior comes! Look, His reward is with Him, and His recompense goes before Him.”
And they will be called the Holy People, the Redeemed of The LORD; and you will be called Sought Out, A City Not Forsaken.

hephzibah.gloss: My delight is in her, a new name replacing Forsaken. It reverses the shame of exile with an intensely personal declaration of God's own pleasure in His people, the same delight language later echoed at Jesus' baptism, this is My beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased, Matthew 3:17.

beulah.gloss: Married, possessed as a wife. Replacing the name Desolate, it pictures the land itself as a bride joined to the LORD, an image of covenant intimacy restored, the same marital metaphor Paul later uses of Christ and the church, Ephesians 5:31-32.

basic.context: A city once called Forsaken and Desolate is given new names, Hephzibah, My Delight Is in Her, and Beulah, Married, pictures of intimate covenant restoration. God commissions watchmen who will not rest until Jerusalem becomes the praise of the earth, and the chapter closes announcing that Zion's Savior comes.
basic.hermeneutics: The renaming is not cosmetic, it describes an actual change in relationship and status, from shame to delight, from desolation to marriage. The watchmen who give God no rest model a kind of prayer that actively partners with God's own stated resolve, verse 1, I will not keep silent.
basic.originalLanguages: The new names, hephtzi-bah, my delight is in her, and be'ulah, married, reverse the shame of Forsaken and Desolate with the language of personal delight and marital intimacy, the same register of delight God uses of His own Son at His baptism.
basic.typology: The bridegroom who rejoices over his bride anticipates Christ's relationship to the church, His bride, and the herald announcing behold, your Savior comes anticipates both the incarnation and Christ's triumphal entry, where crowds prepared His way with garments spread on the road.
basic.christ: The bridegroom imagery of verse 5 finds its full sense in Christ and the church, Ephesians 5:25-27, where Paul describes Christ loving the church as a bridegroom loves a bride, sanctifying her to present her to Himself in splendor. And Matthew 21:5 draws on this chapter's herald language, behold, your king is coming to you, quoted of Jesus' entry into Jerusalem.
basic.god: God refuses to keep silent about His people's shame, actively working and speaking until it is reversed; He is not a distant deity satisfied with mere improvement but a bridegroom who delights personally and specifically in the one He has redeemed.
basic.crossReferences: Ephesians 5:25-27 describes Christ as bridegroom sanctifying the church, His bride. Matthew 21:5 echoes this chapter's herald announcing the coming king. Revelation 21:2 pictures the new Jerusalem prepared as a bride adorned for her husband.
basic.oneStory: A city renamed from Forsaken to My Delight Is in Her, from Desolate to Married, tells the whole Bible's story in miniature, God does not merely rescue His people from shame, He draws them into the closest possible relationship, the intimacy of marriage, fulfilled and consummated when Christ presents His bride, the church, to Himself in splendor.

context.literary: Structured around the renaming motif common in Isaiah's final chapters, moving from God's resolve not to stay silent, through the specific reversal of Zion's names, to a closing commission to prepare the way for a coming salvation, framed by watchmen who echo God's own refusal to rest.
context.historical: Addressed to a community whose identity had been defined by exile's shame, Forsaken, Desolate; the promise of new names functioned as an act of prophetic re-identification, reshaping how the people understood themselves before God even as physical restoration was still unfolding.
context.canonical: Its bridal and marriage language anticipates the New Testament's consistent portrayal of Christ and the church as bridegroom and bride, Ephesians 5, Revelation 19, 21, one of Scripture's most sustained metaphors for covenant relationship.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To replace Zion's identity of shame with an identity of delight and covenant intimacy, and to commission the people to active, persistent prayer, giving God no rest, as partners in seeing that restoration accomplished.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter both promises a change of name and status, and prescribes a response, verses 6-7 explicitly command the watchmen to give God no rest until the promise is fulfilled, modeling persistent intercession as an appropriate response to God's own stated resolve.

debates.question: Are the 'watchmen' of verse 6 human intercessors or angelic or prophetic figures?

views.label: Human intercessors, God's praying people
views.case: The call to take no rest and give God no rest reads naturally as an appeal to human prayer, urging the covenant community to persistent intercession for Jerusalem's full restoration.

views.label: Prophetic watchmen appointed to proclaim, not merely pray
views.case: Elsewhere in Isaiah and Ezekiel, watchmen are prophetic figures tasked with proclamation and warning, suggesting these watchmen may primarily announce rather than only intercede.

debates.bearing: Either reading affirms the same underlying principle: God's people are called to active partnership, whether through prayer or proclamation, in seeing His promises realized, not passive waiting.

study.originalLanguages: Hephtzi-bah, my delight is in her, and be'ulah, married, replace names of shame with names of intimate covenant relationship, reversing Forsaken and Desolate with personal delight and marital belonging, language that finds its deepest register in the Father's declared delight over the Son and the church's marriage to Christ.

typology.type: The LORD rejoicing over Zion as a bridegroom over a bride, verse 5
typology.fulfillment: Christ's love for the church as bridegroom, sanctifying her to present her to Himself in splendor, Ephesians 5:25-27.

typology.type: Prepare the way, your Savior comes, verses 10-11
typology.fulfillment: Jesus' triumphal entry into Jerusalem, Matthew 21:5, and ultimately His incarnation and return.

study.christ: The bridegroom who rejoices over his bride in verse 5 anticipates Christ's own love for the church, whom Paul describes as a bride Christ sanctifies and presents to Himself in splendor, without spot or wrinkle, Ephesians 5:25-27. The herald's cry, behold, your Savior comes, echoes forward into Matthew's description of Jesus' triumphal entry, behold, your king is coming to you, humble and mounted on a donkey, Matthew 21:5, and reaches its final form in Revelation, where the new Jerusalem descends prepared as a bride adorned for her husband, Revelation 21:2.
study.god: God actively refuses silence over His people's shame, working and speaking until it is reversed, and He delights in His redeemed people not with distant approval but with the specific, personal joy of a bridegroom over his bride, the deepest relational metaphor Scripture offers for covenant love.

crossReferences.note: Christ loves the church as bridegroom, sanctifying her to present her to Himself in splendor.
Jesus' triumphal entry echoes this chapter's herald announcing the coming Savior and King.
The new Jerusalem descends prepared as a bride adorned for her husband.
The LORD rejoices over His people with singing, echoing the bridegroom's joy of verse 5.

study.oneStory: A city once named Forsaken and Desolate is renamed My Delight Is in Her and Married, pictures of restored intimacy rather than mere rescue. That marriage imagery runs through the whole Bible's story of covenant love, and finds its consummation in Christ, the bridegroom who loves, sanctifies, and will one day fully present His bride, the church, to Himself.

quiz.q: What do the new names Hephzibah and Beulah replace, and what do they mean?

options.text: They replace Forsaken and Desolate, meaning My Delight Is in Her and Married.
options.note: Verse 4 explicitly gives this contrast and meaning.

options.text: They replace Blessed and Holy with names of judgment.
options.note: The movement is from shame to delight, not from blessing to judgment.

options.text: They are simply geographic place names with no symbolic meaning.
options.note: The names are explicitly symbolic, reversing the meaning of Forsaken and Desolate.

options.text: They describe only a change in the city's physical architecture.
options.note: Rebuilding does appear elsewhere in this section, but these specific names describe relational and covenantal status, not architecture.

quiz.q: What are the watchmen in verses 6-7 commanded to do?

options.text: Never be silent, taking no rest themselves and giving God no rest until Jerusalem is established as the praise of the earth.
options.note: Verses 6-7 give this explicit, persistent commission.

options.text: Guard the city only against physical military attack.
options.note: Watchmen do guard walls elsewhere in Scripture, but here their role is persistent calling on the LORD in prayer, not solely physical defense.

options.text: Remain silent so as not to disturb God's rest.
options.note: The opposite; they are told never to be silent and to give God no rest.

options.text: Take turns resting in shifts with no urgency implied.
options.note: The text emphasizes urgency, they will never be silent day or night.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament apply the bridegroom-and-bride imagery of verse 5?

options.text: Paul applies it to Christ's love for the church, sanctifying her as His bride, Ephesians 5:25-27.
options.note: Ephesians 5 draws directly on this marital covenant imagery.

options.text: It is applied only to human marriages with no theological significance.
options.note: Human marriage does reflect this pattern, but its primary New Testament application is theological, Christ's love for the church.

options.text: The New Testament never uses bridal imagery for God's people.
options.note: Ephesians 5 and Revelation 19, 21 use this imagery extensively.

options.text: It is used to describe the relationship between Israel and pagan nations.
options.note: The bridal imagery describes covenant intimacy between God, or Christ, and His own people, not pagan nations.

quiz.q: What does the herald announce in verse 11, and how does it connect to the Gospels?

options.text: Behold, your Savior comes, echoed in Matthew's description of Jesus' triumphal entry into Jerusalem.
options.note: Matthew 21:5 draws on this kind of herald language for Jesus' entry.

options.text: It announces a military victory with no connection to the New Testament.
options.note: It is directly connected to Christ's coming in the Gospels.

options.text: It predicts the rebuilding of the temple only, unrelated to any messianic figure.
options.note: Rebuilding is a theme elsewhere in this section, but this specific announcement centers on a coming Savior, directly applied to Christ.

options.text: It is addressed only to foreign kings, not to Zion herself.
options.note: It is explicitly addressed to Daughter Zion, telling her that her Savior comes.

questions: Zion was renamed from Forsaken to My Delight Is in Her. What old, shame-based name or identity are you still carrying that God wants to replace with His delight over you?
The watchmen were commanded to give God no rest until His promises were fulfilled. Is there a promise of God you have stopped actively, persistently praying for?
God is pictured as a bridegroom rejoicing over Zion as his bride. Does your relationship with God feel like duty or performance, or does it carry the warmth of being delighted in?
The people were told to prepare the way, clear the stones, and raise a banner for the nations. What practical step could you take this week to prepare the way for someone else to meet God?
Zion's final new name is Sought Out, A City Not Forsaken. Where do you need to remember that God actively seeks you out rather than waiting for you to find your own way back?

translationNotes.note: Hephzibah and Beulah are transliterated Hebrew names rather than translated in most English versions; the BSB gives both the transliteration and the meaning to preserve the wordplay of renaming.
Sought Out translates a rare term implying deliberate, active pursuit, reinforcing that Zion's restoration comes from God's initiative rather than her own effort to be found.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via makor-scripture toolkit for Isaiah 62:1-12; no em dashes present in this passage.
